case_name: State of New Hampshire v. Donald Freese
transcript_text:
This is case 20250028, state of New Hampshire versus Donald

Freese. Good morning. For the record, Justice Will is not able to be with us

physically present today, but he will be reviewing the recording of today's

argument and will participate in our deliberations. Thank you. And Attorney

Hausmann, I understand you reserved two minutes for you. Please proceed. May it

please the court, Stephanie Hausmann, on behalf of Donald Freese. The court's

inherent authority does not permit it to diverge from the course plainly set by

the legislature, even if fraud is suspected. Because the legislature has

made clear what remedy the state has if it discovers that a dismissal may have

resulted from malingering. That dismissal is by law without prejudice. The

plain meaning of these terms, drawn from Black's law dictionary, is that the

required dismissal is not interlocutory, as the state argues, but it terminates

the pending cause of action while allowing the state the ability to

seek re-entitement within the limitations period. The ancient and out-of-state

precedent upon which the state relies in its inherent authority argument does

not relate to and does not rebut the plain language of New Hampshire's

competence scheme. Nor does the Demesman case get the state where it's trying to

go. There were two main elements of the Demesman opinion, one related to

malingering and one related to fidelity to the statutory competence scheme. In

that case, the parties upon Mr. Demesman's finding of not competent had

created sort of a bespoke process by which they intended to proceed with

the competence question. And then the defendant argued that the state was

past the point of no return and so couldn't seek a re-evaluation to

determine whether Mr. Demesman had been malingering. This court said, we don't

care what process the two of you came up with, we look at the statutory

scheme. And under the statutory scheme, Mr. Demesman isn't at the point of

no return. He's well within the court's inherent authority to order a

second competence evaluation to look at the question of malingering. I'm happy to

answer the court's questions. Under the statutory scheme, so the

women on the inherent authority is the plain language of the statute. Absolutely.

Yes. And where do we, where in the authority governing inherent

authority, do you point to for support that this particular statutory

construct limits what would otherwise be within the discretion of the court?

Sure. RSA 13517A paragraph four indicates that if there is a finding that

the defendant has not been restored to competence within 12 months of the

original not competent finding, the case shall be dismissed without

prejudice. And so the court and the parties below recognize that at that

point in time, the case was by law dismissed without prejudice. My

question is slightly different. I understand that language in this

statute, but how do we know that that statute, statutory scheme

inhibits or restricts, you don't dispute that the trial court has

inherent authority generally. Trial courts have a lot of inherent

authority to operate within the construct that the legislature

has given them. Yes. So, so that is the limitation you're pointing to.

And the limitation in this case is that is the limitations of

paragraph four. Yes. Is that right? Yes. Because the legislature has

mandated dismissal without prejudice at that point in time that and

that terminates that litigation and it limits the court's inherent

authority because there is no longer a pending matter before the

court. So, given that statutory scheme, what should have happened

here? Sure. As the state and the both parties and the court

recognize below the state can seek re indictment of a person

who had been had their case dismissed as a result of a

finding of not competent and either not restorable or not

restored. And so the state always has the opportunity to seek

re indictment. And if they secure indictments in a second

litigation, then because of the posture that this case ended in

there is a presumption of incompetence, which merely means

that the state has to bring that matter to the court's

attention and seek a competence determination. So I guess

under the relatively involved facts here, when should when

should the state have sought the re indictment? At what point

in time? Sure. The state could have sought re indictment as soon

as it suspected that the that Mr. Demesment, I mean, Mr.

Freese was not mentally ill in a forensically significant way

as four doctors and at least three judges had found. And so

they are free to seek re indictment at any point in

time. The state's goal was that there be no gap in bail or

bail conditions for Mr. Freeze. And so it was incumbent upon the

state to seek that re indictment at a time at which it could

then assure itself that there would be no gap in court

control over Mr. Freeze. And I assume that would incorporate

any concern about when a grand jury may or may not be

sitting in terms of in terms of the ability to re indict. I

understood that towards the so the court held Mr. Freeze for

up to 90 days for the state to seek civil commitment. And

during that process, early in that process, the state learned

that it would not have the certificate it needed to

advance on civil commitment. That could have been a point

at time at which it sought re indictment. It didn't really

talk seriously about seeking re indictment until towards the end

of the 90 day period. And at that point, it said the grand jury

wouldn't be meeting again. And so there would be a gap. I don't

know the schedule of the grand jury in Hillsborough North, but

imagine that it sits at least once a month. And so didn't

wouldn't expect the gap to be excessive. Should it impact

our analysis that there was a stipulation in 2023 that he

was not competent? Is that weigh in in any way?

I mean, again, at that point, both parties, both attorneys

from both sides, Mr. Freeze insisted at that point in time

that he was competent. But the attorneys from both sides based

on reports from each side forensic psychologist or

expert and the court all agreed that Mr. Freeze was not

competent. And again, I think it is to the extent to this court

needs to determine whether there was a sufficient basis to

believe that Mr. Freeze was malingering. There were at

least four doctors and at least three judges over a 15 month

period who found that Mr. Freeze was significantly mentally

ill, to the extent that it was forensically significant,

rendered him incompetent or rendered him eligible for for

involuntary commitment.

Should the notion that one's competence can potentially come

and go impact the analysis in any way?

I think the legislature has recognized that by giving the

state the ability. It's a dismissal without prejudice. And

so this court has certainly recognized that mental illness

symptoms can wax and wane. And that gives the state the

ability to reinitiate litigation in the future if

that is the case in a particular case.

I'm not sure I heard you correctly in your opening

remarks. Are you conceding that malingering is essentially

fraud?

I think the court needs to determine that. But certainly

the allegation here that the state is arguing is that

malingering is a variant of fraud that needs to be

addressed.

Is it does it rise to a level of fraud on the court? I

mean, it statesites Conant versus O'Mara. Is it is it

is that case applicable in your view in terms of the

level of fraud involved?

No, the quantum of evidence that there was malingering is

is incomplete for this court's review, but also really

controversial. Right? There is this long history. Mr.

Fries is in state custody for 15 months. And the one of

the evaluators or both of the evaluators early on, not

only examined his medical and mental health records, but

examined jail calls. And they both concluded that he

suffered who is operating under this delusional belief

about a conspiracy that he was involved in. That

delusional belief carried through for the 12 to 15

months that competency was litigated. Malingering, as

the court has recognized, is a form of manipulation to

get what you want. But if that's what Mr. Fries

was doing, why in 2023 did he insist on his own

competence? Why in 2023 did he refuse to meet with

the expert that his lawyers had hired? Why in the

probate matter did he testify and say he was not

mentally ill? Why at the restoration hearing did he

testify and say I'm not mentally ill? This is

a under the state's view of the the record. It's

a very long con that they are saying Mr. Fries

engaged in. And so I think the evidence of

malingering is is by no means overwhelming. If we

assume that the trial court had the ability to

reinstate these indictments, you just refer to

the fact that there was no finding of malingering. As

I understand the record, there was simply a

suggestion from one psychiatrist who had previously

found him to be incompetent that he was malingering

and it was based upon that suspicion, and correct me

if I'm wrong, but it was based upon that suspicion

that the indictments were were reinstated. Can

you can you address whether that suspicion

alone based on the testimony of one expert is

sufficient or does the court's exercise of

discretion have to be based upon an evidentiary

finding that in fact there was malingering? I

think the statutory scheme contained in RSA

13517A does not give the trial court any discretion

at this junction. And I understand that. Well

let's assume that that we conclude that there

is discretion in the circumstance. Can that

discretion be exercised without an evidentiary

determination that there was in fact malingering? I

think that the record needs to be much more

fulsome in the trial court on the question of

malingering before the case can proceed and before

this court has an adequate record to decide that

issue. And so if the state were to

re-indite and reignite this litigation then

they bear the presumption is that he's not

incompetent. And the state bears the burden. And

so the state would have to present evidence

that compellingly showed that there was

malingering and explained why that doctor

Dr. Lampagnano had changed his opinion

in a span of six months and address the

question of what Mr. Fries's

the opinion seemed in large part to be based

on Mr. Fries's saying talking about the

delusional belief system he held in some

context and not in others. But again Mr.

Fries explained I'm not going to I don't want

to talk to mental health people because I'm

not mentally ill. And so there was an

explanation for why this delusional belief

system did not present itself. But it's

your position that the court didn't have

the authority to hold that hearing unless

he was re-indicted because the case had

already been dismissed as a matter of

all. That's right. That's right. And

another problem that this situation brings

the court into is that the statute only

gives the state 12 months to restore him

to competence. And so that 12 months is

is long past. But the trial court's

ruling on the state's motion sought to

sort of rewind the clock to that

moment. But the months have been

passing. And so not only is it the

court didn't have discretion at that

point given paragraph four but it

also couldn't bring itself back to

that 12-month period where it had to

restore Mr. Friese to confidence. Is he

still out on bail on his own record?

He is. Yes. Thank you attorney

segments.text: This is case 20250028, state of New Hampshire versus Donald
Freese. Good morning. For the record, Justice Will is not able to be with us
physically present today, but he will be reviewing the recording of today's
argument and will participate in our deliberations. Thank you. And Attorney
Hausmann, I understand you reserved two minutes for you. Please proceed. May it
please the court, Stephanie Hausmann, on behalf of Donald Freese. The court's
inherent authority does not permit it to diverge from the course plainly set by
the legislature, even if fraud is suspected. Because the legislature has
made clear what remedy the state has if it discovers that a dismissal may have
resulted from malingering. That dismissal is by law without prejudice. The
plain meaning of these terms, drawn from Black's law dictionary, is that the
required dismissal is not interlocutory, as the state argues, but it terminates
the pending cause of action while allowing the state the ability to
seek re-entitement within the limitations period. The ancient and out-of-state
precedent upon which the state relies in its inherent authority argument does
not relate to and does not rebut the plain language of New Hampshire's
competence scheme. Nor does the Demesman case get the state where it's trying to
go. There were two main elements of the Demesman opinion, one related to
malingering and one related to fidelity to the statutory competence scheme. In
that case, the parties upon Mr. Demesman's finding of not competent had
created sort of a bespoke process by which they intended to proceed with
the competence question. And then the defendant argued that the state was
past the point of no return and so couldn't seek a re-evaluation to
determine whether Mr. Demesman had been malingering. This court said, we don't
care what process the two of you came up with, we look at the statutory
scheme. And under the statutory scheme, Mr. Demesman isn't at the point of
no return. He's well within the court's inherent authority to order a
second competence evaluation to look at the question of malingering. I'm happy to
answer the court's questions. Under the statutory scheme, so the
women on the inherent authority is the plain language of the statute. Absolutely.
Yes. And where do we, where in the authority governing inherent
authority, do you point to for support that this particular statutory
construct limits what would otherwise be within the discretion of the court?
Sure. RSA 13517A paragraph four indicates that if there is a finding that
the defendant has not been restored to competence within 12 months of the
original not competent finding, the case shall be dismissed without
prejudice. And so the court and the parties below recognize that at that
point in time, the case was by law dismissed without prejudice. My
question is slightly different. I understand that language in this
statute, but how do we know that that statute, statutory scheme
inhibits or restricts, you don't dispute that the trial court has
inherent authority generally. Trial courts have a lot of inherent
authority to operate within the construct that the legislature
has given them. Yes. So, so that is the limitation you're pointing to.
And the limitation in this case is that is the limitations of
paragraph four. Yes. Is that right? Yes. Because the legislature has
mandated dismissal without prejudice at that point in time that and
that terminates that litigation and it limits the court's inherent
authority because there is no longer a pending matter before the
court. So, given that statutory scheme, what should have happened
here? Sure. As the state and the both parties and the court
recognize below the state can seek re indictment of a person
who had been had their case dismissed as a result of a
finding of not competent and either not restorable or not
restored. And so the state always has the opportunity to seek
re indictment. And if they secure indictments in a second
litigation, then because of the posture that this case ended in
there is a presumption of incompetence, which merely means
that the state has to bring that matter to the court's
attention and seek a competence determination. So I guess
under the relatively involved facts here, when should when
should the state have sought the re indictment? At what point
in time? Sure. The state could have sought re indictment as soon
as it suspected that the that Mr. Demesment, I mean, Mr.
Freese was not mentally ill in a forensically significant way
as four doctors and at least three judges had found. And so
they are free to seek re indictment at any point in
time. The state's goal was that there be no gap in bail or
bail conditions for Mr. Freeze. And so it was incumbent upon the
state to seek that re indictment at a time at which it could
then assure itself that there would be no gap in court
